Our results show that this choice may affect the interpretation of AVS, although we are unable to The concordance between the two most discrepant AVS interpretation criteria (Padua and Brisbane) was similar to that reported between AVS and adrenal imaging in a meta-analysis of 38 studies.20 Discordance between AVS and adrenal imaging is commonly attributed to adrenal incidentalomas that may coexist with bilateral hyperplasia, or to small adenomas or unilateral hyperplasia th

or to small adenomas or unilateral hyperplasia that are not detected by imaging.12,20–22 However, discordance may also be caused by the failure or misinterpretation of AVS. Aldosterone hypersecretion,23–26 and even non supp

Aldosterone hypersecretion,23–26 and even non suppressible aldosterone hypersecretion,27 may persist after adrenalectomy on the basis of AVS results. Such outcomes indicate erroneously lateralized AVS. In rare cases, patients have been hormonally cured after adrenalectomy on the side of a typical Conn adenoma, despite AVS indicating bilateral PA or unilateral PA in the opposite side.28,29 Despite these disturbing reports, the clinical consequences of the inaccurate interpretation of AVS are uncertain. Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists can correct

Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists can correct hypertension and hypokalemia in unilateral PA.30 Thus, the main problem arises when a lateralized AVS leads to unjustified surgery because of bilateral or, even more concerning, contralateral disease. Our results show that this possibility may occur with the most lenient selectivity criteria. For Hypertension Peer Review. Do not distribute.

Many authors have attempted to measure diagnostic accuracy of AVS.31,32 However, false negatives may go unnoticed because patients without diagnosed lateralized hypersecretion do not undergo surgery.33 False positives may also go unnoticed because it is very difficult to determine whether PA has been cured after surgery: essential hypertension can persist after adrenalectomy in unilateral PA.34,35 Postoperative suppression tests are therefore n

35 Postoperative suppression tests are therefore necessary to ascertain postoperative cure of PA but are consistently performed in a few reference centers only. Consequently, neither the sensitivity nor the spec

Consequently, neither the sensitivity nor the specificity of AVS can be accurately The same methodological concerns apply to the determination of the best set of interpretation criteria. Theoretically, the optimal SI and LI thresholds should be determined as the strongest predictors of post-surgical cure in a group of patients with PA all operated on, irrespective of their AVS results.6 However, such a study would be unethical. Mulatero et al. propose that the best thresholds a

Mulatero et al. propose that the best thresholds are those that achieve the most reproducible classification in patients subjected to two AVS.9 This predictably leads to the adoption of the strictest set of criteria. Pragmatic considerations also hint toward this direction. Indeed, surgical treatment of bilateral PA is probably less desirable than medical treatment of unilateral PA. Hence, false negatives of AVS are less of a concern than false positives and high diagnostic thresholds seem preferable. Although the best value of the thresholds is unkno

Although the best value of the thresholds is unknown, it is nonetheless problematic that patients with the same AVS results may not receive the same treatment in two different reference centers. Ideally, diagnostic criteria should be standardized across centers. Moreover, it is common practice to take duplicate blood samples during AVS and there is no consensus about what sample should be used or how they should be combined. These factors may further increase the heterogeneity of AVS interpretation. In our study, the strictest For Hypertension Peer

Some limitations of our study need to be highlighted. First, the analysis is retrospective, but the data were recorded prospectively and few values were missing in the electronic database. However, using potent antihypertensive drugs in high-risk patients matches real-life practice and some studies suggest that AVS remains informative in patients taking interfering drugs and even in those taking spironolactone.6,38 Third, we performed bilateral simultaneous AVS whereas the three other centers perform sequential AVS. Fourth, we used a RIA assay for aldosterone measurements and using mass spectrometry may decrease analytical variability and between-sample variability. Fifth, the design of our study does not enable us to provide recommendations about the optimal SI and LI that should be used, or about potential improvements (like using metanephrines instead of cortisol for computing the SI) or alternatives to AVS (like functional imaging with 11C- AVS is currently considered the reference test for assessing the lateralization of aldosterone hypersecretion in primary aldosteronism, and is considered preferable to imaging. However, its results should be interpreted with ca

However, its results should be interpreted with caution because cut-offs determining the success of adrenal sampling and the lateralization of hypersecretion are not standardized. Different criteria currently used in experienced institutions translate into heterogeneous classifications and hence management decisions for PA patients (with discordance in up to 40% of AVS). Moreover, the true specificity and sensitivity of

Moreover, the true specificity and sensitivity of AVS are unknown, and evidence of its superiority over adrenal imaging is methodologically weak due to the lack of independent gold standard. Standardization is a prerequisite to collaborative research and to the evaluation of the diagnostic and prognostic value of AVS. A recent expert consensus statement on the interpr

A recent expert consensus statement on the interpretation of AVS proposed a cut-off value of SI ≥ 2 and LI ≥ 2 under unstimulated conditions and SI ≥ 3 and LI ≥ 4 during cosyntropin stimulation.6 This consensus may allow the interpretation of AVS to be standardized across centers, although the use of cosyntropin, the usefulness of multiple blood samples from the same vein (and the best way to handle them), and even the true diagnostic value of AVS remain uncertain. ACCOUNTANTS AND BUSINESS EXECUTIVES GENERALLY. By W. S. Zimmerman
T LEAST once to every man
comes a time when he is made
the boss of others. Some men fumble
this opportunity; others apparently
with no more ability make it a step­
ping stone to a successful career. What is bossing? Why is one man
able to transform a sluggish group
of workers into spirited enthusiasts,
and why does

of workers into spirited enthusiasts,
and why does another man given the
same chance have to be removed? The first man, we have all seen his
kind, apparently does nothing all day
long except to nod a nonchalant "yes"
or "no" to problems brought to him
for a decision. The second man
works furiously, stays long hours,
keeps in close touch with the work of
his department, but in spite of drudg­
ery he fails. It doesn't seem fair. Bossing, first of all, must conform
to human nature. As it fails to touch
off the instinct of loyalty, waken the
spirit of r

off the instinct of loyalty, waken the
spirit of romance, adventure, or
sportsmanship, so bossing falls short
of its purpose. Modern Pioneers
We are not so very far to-day from
the times of the great explorers, pio­
neers, and fighters on land and sea.
These great captains and their lieu­
tenants developed in mankind the in­
stinct for following a gallant leader. The world is safe for democracy
these days, but the instinct for fol­ lowing the leader, bred into us for
centuries, still persists. It shows it­
self now-a

centuries, still persists. It shows it­
self now-a-days largely in games
which the former fighter-explorer
type plays, or wants to play. But there is another outlet for the
instinct for adventure, romance, fol­
low-the-leader, or whatever you
choose to call it. Business! Business
has opened a new field for adventure
and offers all the opportunity for per­
sonal exploits that old-time adventure
ever did. The Three in a Big Deal
Some people instinctively sense this
opportunity. Many men get as much
satisfaction from closing a big deal
as a fighter

satisfaction from closing a big deal
as a fighter in the good old days got
from laying out a dozen men on a
battlefield. To them business is a
glorious adventure crammed with per­
sonal enjoyment. But to thousands of other men—
the big majority, business is drudg­
ery which must be endured. Through
ignorance, or lacking imagination,
they fritter away their instinct for
personal accomplishment. This is not entirely their fault. For
their so-called leaders instead of de­
veloping a crew of personal follow­
ers, as in old times, permit themselves
to get so

ers, as in old times, permit themselves
to get so wrapped up in their per­
sonal affairs, they overlook the greater
results that would happen by rous- ing an "up-boys-and-at-'em" spirit
among their grudging helpers. Once in a while we hear of men
who have not overlooked this bigger
purpose. Charles M. Schwab, presi­
dent of the United States Steel Cor­
poration, is one of them. The story
of his rise scintillates with tales of
his purposeful inspiring of men
through their sporting instincts, their
natural desire to outdo their fellows,
their desir

natural desire to outdo their fellows,
their desire for personal accomplish­
ment. To illustrate: On a tour of his mills one day,
Schwab asked a furnace man how
many tons of steel his gang had
melted that day. On learning that
it was nine tons he wrote a large fig­
ure "9" on the furnace door. The
night gang saw the figure, inquired
about it, and promptly "got a hump
on." The day gang on returning to
work found the "9" erased and a "10"
in its place. In turn they speeded
up. The spirit was communicated to
other melting gangs, and the output of
each furnac

other melting gangs, and the output of
each furnace was increased from the
old average of nine to a new average
of fourteen tons a shift. But we do not have to go to
Schwab or to other great leaders for
proof of what this play spirit will
do. Production chiefs, sales manag­
ers, office men all over the country
have tested the sportive spirit of their
helpers and found that an appeal to
it gets results. There is no secret
about it. Anyone can do it. The
only wonder is that so few try this
elementary, basic, but sure-fire
method. The "show

elementary, basic, but sure-fire
method. The "show-off spirit" is so strong
in humanity it manifests itself in the
cradle. In irrepressible childhood it
is plain to the most casual observer.
Little girls love to dress up in
mother's clothes and parade the street.
Boys enjoy marbles, run races, play games to prove their superiority over
their fellows. But there seems to exist by com­
mon consent a vast conspiracy among
adults to repress this deep-rooted de­
sire for self-expression. By some
strange malice the pack of us unite
to pull down t

strange malice the pack of us unite
to pull down the man who climbs,
or who tries to climb, above his fel­
lows. The weak man or woman suc­
cumbs and stays on the dead level
of monotony, but his desire to show-
off still remains to burst out in all
kinds of wasteful ways. Occasionally a real leader occurs.
He taps this fount of human power
and by force of it is carried to the
heights. Such men are surrounded by loyal,
enthusiastic, indomitable helpers
whose released powers are responsible
for many of the sensational successes
of American

for many of the sensational successes
of American business. We shall illustrate by just one re­
cent example. In 1911 the Philadel­
phia Rapid Traction Company (the
street car lines) had spent $30,000,000
capital and was near bankruptcy. Its
cars were 95 per cent obsolete, its
power houses inadequate, its roadbeds
junk, its men discouraged and
ashamed of their jobs. One man, Thomas E. Mitten, who
then became president, in eleven years
changed an annual deficit of
$1,000,000 to annual net earnings of
$3,000,000, cut down costs, reduced
accidents, mod

$3,000,000, cut down costs, reduced
accidents, modernized equipment, in­
creased pay, and when a fight for con­
trol of the improved system came up,
the employees, through their associa­
tion, bought 10,000 shares of stock
which was voted for Mitten. That is what leadership will do.
The result, one of thousands not so
great, goes back to the first principle
of bossing, inspiring men. This con­
sists of giving proper vent to the de­
sire "to do" which exists in every
one of us. Three New C. P. A.'s
Three students of the I. A. S.
course have been ad

Three students of the I. A. S.
course have been added to the rap­
idly growing list of those who have
passed the C. P. A. examinations and
obtained their degrees. E. E. Fowler, 1083 Lakepoint Ave­
nue, Grosse Point Park, Mich., was

ably serve to confuse the accounting

"Business is Business," the Little Man
"A battle where 'everything goes,'
Where the only gospel is 'get ahead,'
And never spare friends or foes;
'Slay or be slain,' is the slogan cold,
You must struggle and slash and tear,
For Business

You must struggle and slash and tear,
For Business is Business, a fight for gold,
Where all that you do is fair!" "Business is Business," the Big Man said,
"A battle to make of earth
A place to yield us more wine and bread
More pleasure and joy and mirth;
There are still some bandits and buc­
caneers
Who are jungle-bred beasts of trade,
But their number dwindles with passing
And dead is the code they made!" "Business is Business," the Big Man said,
"But it's something that's more, far
For it makes sweet gardens of deserts
And cities i

For it makes sweet gardens of deserts
And cities it built now roar
Where once the deer and gray wolf ran
From the pioneer's swift advance;
Business is Magic that toils for man;
Business is True Romance." "And those who make it a ruthless fight
Have only themselves to blame
If they feel no whit of the keen delight
In playing the Bigger Game,
The game that calls on the heart and
The best of man's strength and nerve:
"Business is Business," the Big Man said,
"And that Business is to serve!"
